title: Preventing the Breakdown of Tight-Binding Waveguide Optics by Löwdin Orthogonalization
authors: Konrad Tschernig, Florian H. Huber, Janik Wolters, Jasmin Meinecke
abstract: Many advancements in optics have relied on the tight-binding approximation, which simplifies the description and prediction of complex system behaviors. This approximation describes the dynamics of the total light field by examining the coupling between the guided modes of individual single-mode substructures -- also known as coupled mode theory. However, the underlying assumption, that the guided modes of individual waveguides form an orthogonal basis, breaks down when waveguides are brought into close proximity or when larger arrays are considered. In this work, we systematically analyze the consequences of this non-orthogonality and show that it leads to a generalized eigenvalue problem involving an overlap matrix, causing a fundamental mismatch between the standard TB model and solutions of the paraxial wave equation. To resolve this issue, we introduce a modified TB framework based on the Löwdin orthogonalization, which constructs an orthonormal basis from the non-orthogonal guided modes while minimally altering their physical shape and preserving their symmetry properties. The resulting Löwdin-TB method restores the standard eigenvalue problem and yields excellent agreement with exact beam propagation simulations across a wide range of system sizes and waveguide separations. Furthermore, it captures important physical effects, such as enhanced long-range coupling and nontrivial hopping phases, that are absent in the standard approach.
summary: This paper addresses the failure of the standard tight-binding model for coupled optical waveguides when mode overlap is significant. The authors propose using Löwdin orthogonalization to generate an orthonormal basis, which corrects the Hamiltonian and restores the model's accuracy. This improved Löwdin-TB method accurately predicts complex coupling effects, such as negative next-nearest-neighbor hopping phases, previously missed by standard approximations.
main_problem: The standard tight-binding approximation for light propagation in waveguide arrays fails when waveguides are closely spaced because the underlying assumption of mode orthogonality breaks down.
main_result: The proposed Löwdin-TB method successfully restores the standard eigenvalue problem and accurately reproduces exact beam propagation simulations, capturing crucial physical effects like negative next-nearest-neighbor coupling.
method: The authors introduce a modified tight-binding framework using Löwdin orthogonalization to construct an orthonormal basis from the non-orthogonal guided modes.
model_or_system: The system is a linear array of coupled single-mode optical waveguides, whose dynamics are governed by the paraxial wave equation. The model is analyzed using a tight-binding Hamiltonian approach.
key_observables: Fidelity (comparison to BPM), Spectral Distance (S), Completeness Distance (C), and Frobenius Distance (F) are used to quantify the accuracy of the approximation.
important_parameters: Waveguide separation distance ($d$), number of waveguides ($M$), and the overlap matrix elements ($\langle\psi_n|\psi_mangle$).
main_assumptions: The standard TB method assumes guided modes are mutually orthogonal, which is the primary flawed assumption addressed by the work.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the breakdown of standard TB fidelity versus BPM for increasing waveguide count, and plot the modified Hamiltonian elements ($H_{n,m}$) showing the emergence of negative NNN coupling phases in the Löwdin approach.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the failure of standard TB due to non-orthogonality, then introduces the Löwdin orthogonalization procedure, and finally validates the new method by comparing its results against exact beam propagation simulations using multiple quantitative error metrics.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous mathematical tool (Löwdin orthogonalization) to correct a fundamental approximation in coupled-mode theory, which is essential for accurately modeling light-matter or light-waveguide interactions in complex photonic structures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Propagation in different Waveguide Systems Intensity evolution in waveguide arrays with (a) M = 2, (b) M = 5 and (c) M = 25 waveguides, respectively. The exact results are obtained by solving the paraxial wave equation using the beam propagation method (BPM), left panels, while the middle panels show the results of the standard tight-binding method and the right panels the L¨owdin TB method. The red arrows (circle) indicate the input waveguide. In (a) and (b) the waveguides are arranged in a 1D lattice in the x −z-plane and we show the full evolution of the field in the x −z plane (cut at y = 0). In (c) the waveguides are arranged in a 5 × 5 square lattice and we show the field in...

FIG. 3. L¨owdin Tight-binding Method (a) Amplitude distributions |ϕn⟩in the x-y-plane of the L¨owdin modes for two identical coupled waveguides. Red (blue) surfaces correspond to positive (negative) amplitude. (b) Visualization of the hopping integrals in the L¨owdin TB method. The L¨owdin coupling coefficient κn,m = ⟨ϕn| ˆH|ϕm⟩is the volume enclosed by the red (positive contribution) and blue (negative contribution) surface and the x-y plane. In both (a) and (b) the position of the waveguides is indicated by the black circles and the dashed lines are surface contours to enhance the 3D visualization.

FIG. 4. Propagation Constants and Coupling Coefficients in Standard- and L¨owdin-TB We plot the matrix elements Hn,m of a linear array of 10 identical, coupled waveguides (see in-sets) as a function of the nearest neighbor distance d for (a) m = n (effective propagation constants), (b) m = n + 1 (nearest neighbor coupling), (c) m = n + 2 (next-nearest neighbor coupling) and (d) m = n + 3 (next-next-nearest neighbor coupling). The blue (red) lines show the Standard-TB (L¨owdin-TB) Hamiltonian. FIG. 5. Comparison of the Accuracy of the Standard and L¨owdin TB Methods We plot the (a) Spectral distance S, (b) Completeness distance C and (c) the Frobenius distance F to the exact eigenmode expansion, according to Eqs. (10-12), of the Standard TB method (dashed lines) and L¨owdin TB method (solid lines) as a function of the waveguide lattice constant d. The line colors correspond to 1D arrays with M = 2 waveguides (blue) and M = 5 waveguides (red) and a 2D array with M = 25 waveguides (yellow).
